ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Sichuan Mouthwatering Chicken (Kou Shui Ji). The closest parent 'chicken food product' (FOODON_00002670) is used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Sichuan-style poached chicken in chilli oil. 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A00GJ) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955001 'Chicken dish (food)' is the closest available parent concept; no specific code exists for Kou Shui Ji.

preparation_method: garnished with sesame paste and Sichuan peppercorn

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Sichuan Mouthwatering Chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising chilled poached chicken pieces generously dressed in chilli oil sauce, sesame paste, and Sichuan peppercorn aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted — adapted with sauce composition adjustment for Sichuan Kou Shui Ji preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Foundation Foods / SR Legacy, supplemented by peer-reviewed compositional analysis of Sichuan chilli oil sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Kou Shui Ji exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken (FDC ID 171477) provides the protein, fat, and energy base. Sauce components (chilli oil, sesame paste, soy sauce, peanuts) contribute additional fat, sodium, and carbohydrate. Final per-100g values are estimated by blending cooked chicken (~65% by weight) with sauce (~35% by weight) using standard compositional data. This is an adapted estimate; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chicken and protein-dominant dishes with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sichuan Mouthwatering Chicken (Kou Shui Ji). GI is estimated at 15 based on analogous protein-dominant poultry dishes with chilli oil sauce and minimal carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100g). Protein-dominant dishes with low carbohydrate load consistently yield GI values in the 10–20 range per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (15 × 10.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and chilli oil sauce (~480 mg sodium per 100g; ~1440 mg per 300g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150g or less and request reduced soy sauce, as a standard 300g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium — nearly 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100g) with high protein supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sichuan Mouthwatering Chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low GI and minimal carbohydrate load; however, the high sodium content from the sauce warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (12 g per 100g) with relatively favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, but high sodium and chilli oil content raise cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, as the high sodium content (~1440 mg per serving) and chilli oil-derived fat may exacerbate blood pressure and lipid management goals; the favourable unsaturated fat profile from sesame and peanut components partially offsets saturated fat concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sichuan Mouthwatering Chicken, known in Chinese as 口水鸡 (Kǒu Shuǐ Jī), is a classic cold dish originating from Sichuan province in China. The name literally translates to 'saliva chicken', evoking the mouth-watering effect of its intensely flavoured sauce. The dish is prepared by poaching a whole chicken or chicken pieces in seasoned water until just cooked through, then chilling the meat before slicing and dressing it generously with a complex sauce comprising Sichuan chilli oil, sesame paste, soy sauce, black vinegar, sugar, minced garlic, ginger, and ground Sichuan peppercorn. The result is a dish with a striking combination of flavours: numbing (má), spicy (là), savoury, slightly sweet, and tangy — the hallmark profile of Sichuan cuisine. The texture of the chicken is silky and tender, contrasting with the rich, oily, and aromatic sauce. Garnishes typically include toasted sesame seeds, crushed peanuts, and sliced spring onions. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (~16.5 g per 100g) and moderate in fat (~12 g per 100g), with the fat profile skewed toward unsaturated fats from sesame and peanut components. Carbohydrate content is very low (~3.5 g per 100g), yielding a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100g; ~1440 mg per 300g serving) due to soy sauce and chilli oil, which is a significant concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy. In Singapore, this dish is served at Sichuan-style Chinese restaurants and is popular as a starter or shared cold dish. The restaurant variant at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant incorporates fresh chilli (鲜椒) in addition to chilli oil, adding brightness and heat to the traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: What is the calorie count of Sichuan cold chicken?
Is mouthwatering chicken suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Sichuan Mouthwatering Chicken (Kou Shui Ji) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA cooked chicken data combined with estimated sauce component contributions. GI is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant poultry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted estimation methodology.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source for cooked chicken and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where compositional data was available. Nutrient values represent an adapted estimate for the composite dish (poached chicken + Sichuan chilli oil sauce) and should be validated against direct laboratory analysis if used for clinical or regulatory purposes. The restaurant variant (Wan Jia Fu, Circular Road, Singapore) incorporates fresh chilli (鲜椒) which may slightly alter the sodium and capsaicin content relative to the standard preparation. Halal status is not confirmed as the restaurant's certification status is unknown. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous protein-dominant poultry dishes with low carbohydrate content per University of Sydney GI Database methodology; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).
